threats.name: node-fetch forwards secure headers to untrusted sites
threats.description: node-fetch forwards secure headers such as `authorization`, `www-authenticate`, `cookie`, & `cookie2` when redirecting to a untrusted site.

threats.name: Next.js Server-Side Request Forgery in Server Actions
threats.description:
### Impact
A Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability was identified in Next.js Server Actions by security researchers at Assetnote. If the `Host` header is modified, and the below conditions are also met, an attacker may be able to make requests that appear to be originating from the Next.js application server itself.

#### Prerequisites
* Next.js (`<14.1.1`) is running in a self-hosted* manner.
* The Next.js application makes use of Server Actions.
* The Server Action performs a redire

threats.name: jsonwebtoken unrestricted key type could lead to legacy keys usage
threats.description:
# Overview

Versions `<=8.5.1` of `jsonwebtoken` library could be misconfigured so that legacy, insecure key types are used for signature verification. For example, DSA keys could be used with the RS256 algorithm. 

# Am I affected?

You are affected if you are using an algorithm and a key type other than the combinations mentioned below

| Key type |  algorithm                                    |
|----------|------------------------------------------|
| ec           | ES256, ES384, ES512

threats.name: Next.js Cache Poisoning
threats.description:
### Impact

By sending a crafted HTTP request, it is possible to poison the cache of a non-dynamic server-side rendered route in the pages router (this does not affect the app router). When this crafted request is sent it could coerce Next.js to cache a route that is meant to not be cached and send a `Cache-Control: s-maxage=1, stale-while-revalidate` header which some upstream CDNs may cache as well. 

To be potentially affected all of the following must apply: 

- Next.js between 13.5.1 and 14

threats.name: axios Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity vulnerability
threats.description: axios before v0.21.2 is vulnerable to Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity.

threats.name: Command Injection in lodash
threats.description: `lodash` versions prior to 4.17.21 are vulnerable to Command Injection via the template function.

threats.name: Next.js authorization bypass vulnerability
threats.description:
### Impact
If a Next.js application is performing authorization in middleware based on pathname, it was possible for this authorization to be bypassed.

### Patches
This issue was patched in Next.js `14.2.15` and later.

If your Next.js application is hosted on Vercel, this vulnerability has been automatically mitigated, regardless of Next.js version.

### Workarounds
There are no official workarounds for this vulnerability.

#### Credits
We'd like to thank [tyage](http://github.com/tyage) (GMO

threats.name: axios Requests Vulnerable To Possible SSRF and Credential Leakage via Absolute URL
threats.description:
### Summary

A previously reported issue in axios demonstrated that using protocol-relative URLs could lead to SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery). Reference: axios/axios#6463

A similar problem that occurs when passing absolute URLs rather than protocol-relative URLs to axios has been identified. Even if ⁠`baseURL` is set, axios sends the request to the specified absolute URL, potentially causing SSRF and credential leakage. This issue impacts both server-side and client-side usage of axios.

##

threats.name: Next Vulnerable to Denial of Service with Server Components
threats.description:
A vulnerability affects certain React packages for versions 19.0.0, 19.0.1, 19.1.0, 19.1.1, 19.1.2, 19.2.0, and 19.2.1 and frameworks that use the affected packages, including Next.js 15.x and 16.x using the App Router. The issue is tracked upstream as [CVE-2025-55184](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2025-55184).

A malicious HTTP request can be crafted and sent to any App Router endpoint that, when deserialized, can cause the server process to hang and consume CPU. This can result in denia

threats.name: Next has a Denial of Service with Server Components - Incomplete Fix Follow-Up
threats.description:
It was discovered that the fix for [CVE-2025-55184](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-2m3v-v2m8-q956) in React Server Components was incomplete and did not fully mitigate denial-of-service conditions across all payload types.  As a result, certain crafted inputs could still trigger excessive resource consumption. 

This vulnerability affects React versions 19.0.2, 19.1.3, and 19.2.2, as well as frameworks that bundle or depend on these versions, including Next.js 13.x, 14.x, 15.x, and 16.x when

threats.name: Next.js HTTP request deserialization can lead to DoS when using insecure React Server Components
threats.description:
A vulnerability affects certain React Server Components packages for versions 19.0.x, 19.1.x, and 19.2.x and frameworks that use the affected packages, including Next.js 13.x, 14.x, 15.x, and 16.x using the App Router. The issue is tracked upstream as [CVE-2026-23864](https://github.com/facebook/react/security/advisories/GHSA-83fc-fqcc-2hmg).

A specially crafted HTTP request can be sent to any App Router Server Function endpoint that, when deserialized, may trigger excessive CPU usage, out-of-m

threats.name: Axios is Vulnerable to Denial of Service via __proto__ Key in mergeConfig
threats.description:
# Denial of Service via **proto** Key in mergeConfig

### Summary

The `mergeConfig` function in axios crashes with a TypeError when processing configuration objects containing `__proto__` as an own property. An attacker can trigger this by providing a malicious configuration object created via `JSON.parse()`, causing complete denial of service.

### Details

The vulnerability exists in `lib/core/mergeConfig.js` at lines 98-101:

```javascript
utils.forEach(Object.keys({ ...config1, ...config2 }

threats.name: Authorization Bypass in Next.js Middleware
threats.description:
# Impact
It is possible to bypass authorization checks within a Next.js application, if the authorization check occurs in middleware.

# Patches
* For Next.js 15.x, this issue is fixed in `15.2.3`
* For Next.js 14.x, this issue is fixed in `14.2.25`
* For Next.js 13.x, this issue is fixed in 13.5.9
* For Next.js 12.x, this issue is fixed in 12.3.5
* For Next.js 11.x, consult the below workaround.

_Note: Next.js deployments hosted on Vercel are automatically protected against this vulnerability.

threats.name: lodash vulnerable to Code Injection via `_.template` imports key names
threats.description:
### Impact

The fix for [CVE-2021-23337](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-35jh-r3h4-6jhm) added validation for the `variable` option in `_.template` but did not apply the same validation to `options.imports` key names. Both paths flow into the same `Function()` constructor sink.

When an application passes untrusted input as `options.imports` key names, an attacker can inject default-parameter expressions that execute arbitrary code at template compilation time.

Additionally, `_.template` use

threats.name: Next.js has a Denial of Service with Server Components
threats.description:
A vulnerability affects certain React Server Components packages for versions 19.x and frameworks that use the affected packages, including Next.js 13.x, 14.x, 15.x, and 16.x using the App Router. The issue is tracked upstream as [CVE-2026-23869](https://github.com/facebook/react/security/advisories/GHSA-479c-33wc-g2pg). You can read more about this advisory our [this changelog](https://vercel.com/changelog/summary-of-cve-2026-23869).

A specially crafted HTTP request can be sent to any App Rout

threats.name: Axios: Incomplete Fix for CVE-2025-62718 — NO_PROXY Protection Bypassed via RFC 1122 Loopback Subnet (127.0.0.0/8) in Axios 1.15.0
threats.description:
**1. Executive Summary**
This report documents an **incomplete security patch** for the previously disclosed vulnerability **GHSA-3p68-rc4w-qgx5 (CVE-2025-62718)**, which affects the `NO_PROXY` hostname resolution logic in the Axios HTTP library.

**Background — The Original Vulnerability**
The original vulnerability (GHSA-3p68-rc4w-qgx5) disclosed that Axios did not normalize hostnames before comparing them against `NO_PROXY` rules. Specifically, a request to `http://localhost./` (with a traili

threats.name: Axios: Prototype Pollution Gadgets - Response Tampering, Data Exfiltration, and Request Hijacking
threats.description:
## Summary

When `Object.prototype` has been polluted by any co-dependency with keys that axios reads without a `hasOwnProperty` guard, an attacker can (a) silently intercept and modify every JSON response before the application sees it, or (b) fully hijack the underlying HTTP transport, gaining access to request credentials, headers, and body. The precondition is prototype pollution from a separate source in the same process -- lodash < 4.17.21, or any of several other common npm packages with

threats.name: Axios: Header Injection via Prototype Pollution
threats.description:
### Summary

A prototype pollution gadget exists in the Axios HTTP adapter (lib/adapters/http.js) that allows an attacker to inject arbitrary HTTP headers into outgoing requests. The vulnerability exploits duck-type checking of the data payload, where if Object.prototype is polluted with getHeaders, append, pipe, on, once, and Symbol.toStringTag, Axios misidentifies any plain object payload as a FormData instance and calls the attacker-controlled getHeaders() function, merging the returned heade

threats.name: Sequelize vulnerable to SQL Injection via replacements
threats.description:
### Impact

The SQL injection exploit is related to replacements. Here is such an example: 

In the following query, some parameters are passed through replacements, and some are passed directly through the `where` option.

```typescript
User.findAll({
  where: or(
    literal('soundex("firstName") = soundex(:firstName)'),
    { lastName: lastName },
  ),
  replacements: { firstName },
})
```

This is a very legitimate use case, but this query was vulnerable to SQL injection due to how Sequelize

threats.name: Sequelize - Default support for “raw attributes” when using parentheses
threats.description:
### Impact

Sequelize 6.28.2 and prior has a dangerous feature where using parentheses in the attribute option would make Sequelize use the string as-is in the SQL

```ts
User.findAll({
  attributes: [
    ['count(id)', 'count']
  ]
});
```

Produced

```sql
SELECT count(id) AS "count" FROM "users"
```

### Patches

This feature was deprecated in Sequelize 5, and using it prints a deprecation warning.

This issue has been patched in [`@sequelize/core@7.0.0.alpha-20`](https://github.com/sequelize

threats.name: automattic/mongoose vulnerable to Prototype pollution via Schema.path
threats.description: Mongoose is a MongoDB object modeling tool designed to work in an asynchronous environment. Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. The `Schema.path()` function is vulnerable to prototype pollution when setting the schema object. This vulnerability allows modification of the Object prototype and could be manipulated into a Denial of Service (DoS) attack.

threats.name: Mongoose Prototype Pollution vulnerability
threats.description: Prototype Pollution in GitHub repository automattic/mongoose prior to 7.3.3, 6.11.3, and 5.13.20.

threats.name: Mongoose Vulnerable to Prototype Pollution in Schema Object
Multer vulnerable to Denial of Service from maliciously crafted requests
threats.description:
### Impact
A vulnerability in Multer versions >=1.4.4-lts.1 allows an attacker to trigger a Denial of Service (DoS) by sending a malformed multi-part upload request. This request causes an unhandled exception, leading to a crash of the process.

### Patches
Users should upgrade to `2.0.0`

### Workarounds
None

### References

- https://github.com/expressjs/multer/issues/1176
- https://github.com/expressjs/multer/commit/2c8505f207d923dd8de13a9f93a4563e59933665

threats.name: Multer vulnerable to Denial of Service via unhandled exception
threats.description:
### Impact

A vulnerability in Multer versions >=1.4.4-lts.1, <2.0.1 allows an attacker to trigger a Denial of Service (DoS) by sending an upload file request with an empty string field name. This request causes an unhandled exception, leading to a crash of the process.

### Patches

Users should upgrade to `2.0.1`

### Workarounds

None

### References

https://github.com/expressjs/multer/commit/35a3272b611945155e046dd5cef11088587635e9
https://github.com/expressjs/multer/issues/1233
https://git

threats.name: Multer vulnerable to Denial of Service via memory leaks from unclosed streams
threats.description:
### Impact

Multer <2.0.0 is vulnerable to a resource exhaustion and memory leak issue due to improper stream handling. When the HTTP request stream emits an error, the internal `busboy` stream is not closed, violating Node.js stream safety guidance.

This leads to unclosed streams accumulating over time, consuming memory and file descriptors. Under sustained or repeated failure conditions, this can result in denial of service, requiring manual server restarts to recover. All users of Multer han

threats.name: Multer vulnerable to Denial of Service via unhandled exception from malformed request
threats.description:
### Impact

A vulnerability in Multer versions >= 1.4.4-lts.1, < 2.0.2 allows an attacker to trigger a Denial of Service (DoS) by sending a malformed request. This request causes an unhandled exception, leading to a crash of the process.

### Patches

Users should upgrade to `2.0.2`

### Workarounds

None

threats.name: Mongoose search injection vulnerability
threats.description: Mongoose versions prior to 8.8.3, 7.8.3, 6.13.5, and 5.13.23 are vulnerable to improper use of the $where operator. This vulnerability arises from the ability of the $where clause to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in MongoDB queries, potentially leading to code injection attacks and unauthorized access or manipulation of database data.

threats.name: Multer vulnerable to Denial of Service via incomplete cleanup
threats.description:
### Impact

A vulnerability in Multer versions < 2.1.0 allows an attacker to trigger a Denial of Service (DoS) by sending malformed requests, potentially causing resource exhaustion.

### Patches

Users should upgrade to `2.1.0`

### Workarounds

None

threats.name: Multer vulnerable to Denial of Service via resource exhaustion
threats.description:
### Impact

A vulnerability in Multer versions < 2.1.0 allows an attacker to trigger a Denial of Service (DoS) by dropping connection during file upload, potentially causing resource exhaustion.

### Patches

Users should upgrade to `2.1.0`

### Workarounds

None

threats.name: Multer Vulnerable to Denial of Service via Uncontrolled Recursion
threats.description:
### Impact

A vulnerability in Multer versions < 2.1.1 allows an attacker to trigger a Denial of Service (DoS) by sending malformed requests, potentially causing stack overflow.

### Patches

Users should upgrade to `2.1.1`

### Workarounds

None

### Resources

- https://github.com/expressjs/multer/security/advisories/GHSA-5528-5vmv-3xc2
- https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-3520
- https://github.com/expressjs/multer/commit/7e66481f8b2e6c54b982b34c152479e096ce2752
- https://cna.openjsf.or

threats.name: Sequelize v6 Vulnerable to SQL Injection via JSON Column Cast Type
threats.description:
### Summary

SQL injection via unescaped cast type in JSON/JSONB `where` clause processing. The `_traverseJSON()` function splits JSON path keys on `::` to extract a cast type, which is interpolated raw into `CAST(... AS <type>)` SQL. An attacker who controls JSON object keys can inject arbitrary SQL and exfiltrate data from any table.

Affected: v6.x through 6.37.7. v7 (`@sequelize/core`) is not affected.

### Details

In `src/dialects/abstract/query-generator.js`, `_traverseJSON()` extracts a

threats.name: Mongoose search injection vulnerability
threats.description:
Mongoose versions prior to 8.9.5, 7.8.4, and 6.13.6 are vulnerable to improper use of the `$where` operator. This vulnerability arises from the ability of the `$where` clause to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in MongoDB queries, potentially leading to code injection attacks and unauthorized access or manipulation of database data.

NOTE: this issue exists because of an incomplete fix for CVE-2024-53900.

threats.name: Unsafe fall-through in getWhereConditions
threats.description:
### Impact

Providing an invalid value to the `where` option of a query caused Sequelize to ignore that option instead of throwing an error. 

A finder call like the following did not throw an error:

```ts
User.findAll({
  where: new Date(),
});
```

As this option is typically used with plain javascript objects, be aware that this only happens at the top level of this option.

### Patches

This issue has been patched in [`sequelize@6.28.1`](https://github.com/sequelize/sequelize/pull/15699) &

threats.name: Mongoose's Improper Sanitization of $nor in sanitizeFilter May Allow NoSQL Injection
threats.description:
### Impact

This vulnerability allows bypassing Mongoose’s sanitizeFilter query sanitization mechanism via the `$nor` operator.

When sanitizeFilter is enabled, Mongoose wraps query operators in `$eq` to neutralize them. However, prior to the fix, `$nor` was not included in the set of logical operators that are recursively sanitized. Because `$nor` accepts an array (like `$and` and `$or`), and arrays do not trigger `hasDollarKeys()`, malicious operators such as `$ne`, `$gt`, or `$regex` could be

threats.name: Nuxt allows DOS via cache poisoning with payload rendering response
threats.description:
### Summary

By sending a crafted HTTP request to a server behind an CDN, it is possible in some circumstances to poison the CDN cache and highly impacts the availability of a site.

It is possible to craft a request, such as `https://mysite.com/?/_payload.json` which will be rendered as JSON. If the CDN in front of a Nuxt site ignores the query string when determining whether to cache a route, then this JSON response could be served to future visitors to the site.

### Impact

An attacker can p
threats.ruleContext: Crafted payload URLs can be cached by CDNs as JSON, causing denial-of-service or poisoning cached representations.
mustLines: Configure CDN cache keys to respect query strings where payloads alter representations.
Upgrade Nuxt to a patched release that closes unsafe payload rendering paths.

threats.name: FastAPI Guard Auth Bypass via X-Forwarded-For
threats.description: Attacker manipulates X-Forwarded-For header to bypass IP-based access controls. Fixed in fastapi-guard 2.0.0.
threats.ruleHint: Validate X-Forwarded-For using Python ipaddress module. Never trust raw header for access control.
threats.ruleContext: Attackers can spoof X-Forwarded-For to bypass naive IP allowlists in front of FastAPI apps.
mustLines: Never use raw X-Forwarded-For as the sole authorization signal.
Normalize and validate forwarded IPs with Python ipaddress and a trusted proxy configuration.

threats.name: FastAPI Guard regex bypass — XSS/SQLi through middleware
threats.description: Bounded regex in fastapi-guard 3.0.1 bypassed with payloads exceeding length limits.
threats.ruleHint: Never rely solely on regex middleware. Always use Pydantic model validation as primary defence.
threats.ruleContext: Regex-only middleware was bypassed with oversized payloads, allowing XSS/SQLi-class abuse.
mustLines: Do not rely on regex middleware alone for injection defense.
Treat Pydantic models as the primary request boundary and validation layer.

threats.name: FastAPI IDOR — route missing authorization check (found by Claude Code)
threats.description: FastAPI routes with resource ID path parameters had no ownership check. validate_project_access was a no-op stub.
threats.ruleHint: Every route with path parameter must scope DB query through current_user.
threats.ruleContext: Resource IDs in routes were accepted without ownership checks, enabling IDOR across tenants.
mustLines: Authorize every handler that accepts a resource identifier.
Scope database queries through current_user (or equivalent); never fetch by id alone.

threats.name: FastAPI CSRF via text/plain content-type bypass
threats.description: FastAPI <0.65.2 accepted JSON from text/plain requests enabling CSRF attacks without preflight.
threats.ruleHint: Use fastapi>=0.65.2. Never use cookie-only auth without CSRF validation.
threats.ruleContext: Older FastAPI accepted JSON bodies submitted as text/plain, weakening CSRF preflight assumptions.
mustLines: Upgrade FastAPI to a release that closes the text/plain JSON bypass.
Avoid cookie-only authentication without CSRF defenses on mutating routes.

threats.name: FastAPI unauthenticated RCE via code eval endpoint (Langflow)
threats.description: FastAPI app executed arbitrary Python via /api/v1/validate/code without authentication. Actively exploited in wild.
threats.ruleHint: Never expose code execution endpoints without auth. Never eval() user input.
threats.ruleContext: An unauthenticated code-validation endpoint allowed arbitrary Python execution via Langflow integration.
mustLines: Never expose remote code execution or validation endpoints without strong authentication.
Never eval(), exec(), or dynamically run untrusted Python from HTTP payloads.

threats.name: Django ReDoS in strip_tags()
threats.description: Denial of service via catastrophic backtracking in django.utils.html.strip_tags() with crafted input.
threats.ruleHint: Pin django>=4.2.17. Never use strip_tags() on untrusted input without length limiting.

threats.name: Django urlize() XSS
threats.description: django.utils.html.urlize() did not escape certain HTML characters, leading to XSS.
threats.ruleHint: Pin django>=4.2.16. Never use urlize() on untrusted content without |escape.

threats.name: Django URLValidator ReDoS
threats.description: URLValidator susceptible to ReDoS with specially crafted URLs.
threats.ruleHint: Pin django>=4.2.14. Pre-validate URL length before URLValidator.

threats.name: Django DEBUG=True in production exposes stack traces and secrets
threats.description: AI coding tools consistently generate settings.py with DEBUG=True, exposing SQL queries, environment variables, and stack traces.
threats.ruleHint: Always set DEBUG = os.getenv(DJANGO_DEBUG, False) == True. Never hardcode DEBUG=True.

threats.name: Django mass assignment via fields=__all__ in ModelForm
threats.description: AI generates ModelForms with fields=__all__ allowing attackers to set any model field including is_admin, is_staff.
threats.ruleHint: Never use fields=__all__. Always explicitly list permitted fields in ModelForm and DRF serializers.

threats.name: Django IDOR via missing current_user scoping
threats.description: AI generates views using Model.objects.get(pk=pk) without scoping to request.user, enabling any authenticated user to access any record.
threats.ruleHint: Always scope queryset: request.user.models.get(pk=pk). Never use Model.objects.get(pk=pk) in authenticated views.

threats.name: Rails ReDoS in Accept header parsing
threats.description: Action Dispatch vulnerable to ReDoS via crafted Accept headers.
threats.ruleHint: Pin rails>=7.1.3.2. Never manually parse Accept headers.

threats.name: Rails ReDoS in HTTP Token authentication
threats.description: HTTP Token authentication parsing vulnerable to ReDoS.
threats.ruleHint: Pin rails>=7.1.3.2. Use Devise or Rodauth for authentication.

threats.name: Rails mass assignment via permit!
threats.description: AI generates params.permit! or permit(:all) allowing mass assignment of protected attributes like admin, role.
threats.ruleHint: Never use params.permit! Always explicitly list: params.require(:user).permit(:name,:email).

threats.name: Rails IDOR via missing current_user scope
threats.description: AI generates controller actions finding records by bare ID without scoping to current_user.
threats.ruleHint: Never use Model.find(params[:id]) in authenticated controllers. Always scope: current_user.models.find(params[:id]).

threats.name: Go net/netip IPv4-mapped IPv6 misclassification — SSRF bypass
threats.description: Go stdlib misclassified IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses as loopback/private, enabling SSRF and IP allowlist bypasses.
threats.ruleHint: Pin go>=1.22.4 in go.mod. Never use net.IP.IsLoopback() for security decisions without IPv6 normalization.

threats.name: Go HTTP/2 rapid reset DoS
threats.description: HTTP/2 server vulnerable to rapid stream reset attack causing denial of service.
threats.ruleHint: Pin golang.org/x/net>=0.17.0. Set http.Server ReadHeaderTimeout and WriteTimeout.

threats.name: Go SQL injection via fmt.Sprintf in queries
threats.description: AI generates SQL queries using fmt.Sprintf with user input — safe for integers but dangerous for strings.
threats.ruleHint: Never build SQL with fmt.Sprintf. Always use parameterized: db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?", email).

threats.name: Go hardcoded secrets in source
threats.description: AI embeds database passwords, API keys, and JWT secrets directly in Go source files.
threats.ruleHint: Always use os.Getenv() or viper with env binding. Use godotenv in development only.

threats.name: iOS RTKit memory corruption — actively exploited in the wild
threats.description: Memory corruption in RTKit exploited in the wild. Requires iOS 17.4+.
threats.ruleHint: Always target latest iOS SDK. Set MinimumOSVersion to exclude vulnerable versions.

threats.name: iOS sensitive data stored in UserDefaults (unencrypted)
threats.description: AI stores tokens, passwords, and PII in UserDefaults which is unencrypted and accessible via device backup.
threats.ruleHint: Never store credentials in UserDefaults. Always use Keychain with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly.

threats.name: iOS missing certificate pinning — MITM vulnerable
threats.description: AI generates URLSession networking without certificate pinning.
threats.ruleHint: Always implement certificate pinning via URLSessionDelegate. Use TrustKit library for management.

threats.name: Android sensitive data in SharedPreferences (plaintext)
threats.description: AI stores auth tokens in plaintext SharedPreferences, accessible to rooted devices and backups.
threats.ruleHint: Never use SharedPreferences for sensitive data. Always use EncryptedSharedPreferences with MasterKey.Builder.

threats.name: Android missing network_security_config.xml
threats.description: AI generates apps without network security config, allowing cleartext traffic and missing certificate pinning.

threats.name: Android Bluetooth permission bypass
threats.description: Missing permission check in Bluetooth stack.
threats.ruleHint: Always runtime-check Bluetooth permissions with ActivityCompat.checkSelfPermission() before every operation.
